claim: Anthropic's official Claude Code devcontainer Dockerfile uses node:20 as its base, installs Claude Code via npm i -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code, runs as the non-root 'node' user, and keeps the tool set intentionally minimal (git, gh, jq, fzf, zsh, iptables for a firewall script).
quote: FROM node:20 ... apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends less git procps sudo fzf zsh man-db unzip gnupg2 gh iptables ipset iproute2 dnsutils aggregate jq nano vim ... RUN npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@${CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION}

claim: HolyClaude uses node:22-bookworm-slim as its base and a single-stage build, creating a 'claude' user with passwordless sudo and installing Claude Code via the native installer at claude.ai/install.sh.
quote: FROM node:22-bookworm-slim ... RUN usermod -l claude -d /home/claude -m node && groupmod -n claude node && echo "claude ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" > /etc/sudoers.d/claude ... RUN curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash

claim: Playwright's supported Docker path is `npx playwright install --with-deps chromium firefox webkit`, which downloads browser binaries plus every system lib (libnss3, libasound2, libatk-bridge2.0, libgbm, libxkbcommon, etc.); Firefox and WebKit are glibc-only, so Alpine/musl bases are unsupported.
quote: Browser builds for Firefox and WebKit are built for the glibc library. Alpine Linux and other distributions that are based on the musl standard library are not supported.

claim: Microsoft's reference Playwright Docker image is ubuntu:noble with Node.js 24 from NodeSource, and relies on `playwright-core install --with-deps` to bake in all three browsers plus their system deps under /ms-playwright — it does not enumerate apt packages by hand.
quote: FROM ubuntu:noble ... playwright-core install --with-deps ... PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH=/ms-playwright

claim: Debian 12 bookworm is officially supported by Playwright for Chromium, Firefox and WebKit on both x86_64 and arm64, making debian:bookworm-slim or node:22-bookworm-slim viable bases for a full-browser image.
quote: Playwright supports Debian 12 Bookworm on both x86_64 and arm64 for Chromium, Firefox and WebKit.

claim: For .NET SDK 9 in a non-.NET base image, Microsoft documents two clean paths: copy from `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:9.0-bookworm-slim` in a multi-stage build, or run `curl -sSL https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh | bash /dev/stdin -Channel 9.0 -InstallDir /usr/share/dotnet` and symlink to /usr/bin/dotnet.
quote: curl -sSL https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh | bash /dev/stdin -Channel 10.0 -Runtime dotnet -InstallDir /usr/share/dotnet && ln -s /usr/share/dotnet/dotnet /usr/bin/dotnet

claim: dotnet-install.sh requires bash plus ca-certificates, curl, libc6, libgcc-s1, libicu74, libssl3t64, libstdc++6 and tzdata on Debian/Ubuntu; these must be installed before running the script because dotnet-install does not pull system dependencies itself.
quote: apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends ca-certificates curl libc6 libgcc-s1 libicu74 libssl3t64 libstdc++6 tzdata

claim: The Astral-recommended uv install pattern in Docker is to copy the static binary from the distroless image — `COPY --from=ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:0.11.7 /uv /uvx /bin/` — which avoids needing curl+certs in the base and is reproducible when pinned to a tag or digest.
quote:
FROM python:3.12-slim-trixie
COPY --from=ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:latest /uv /uvx /bin/

claim: nvm inside Docker is a known PATH trap: each RUN opens a fresh non-interactive, non-login shell that does not source ~/.bashrc, so nvm's PATH munging is lost — fixes are to chain install+use in one RUN with `. $NVM_DIR/nvm.sh`, set SHELL to a login bash, or export NODE_PATH/PATH via ENV after install.
quote: PATH wrong (node not found) when running container-image with non-interactive shell like in Gitlab CICD-Jobs

claim: The community 'claude-office-skills' toolbelt nails the doc-conversion trio: LibreOffice's `soffice` for PPTX->PDF, Poppler's `pdftoppm` for PDF->image rasterization, and Pandoc for DOCX text extraction preserving tracked changes — paired with python-pptx, openpyxl, pypdf, Pillow, pdf2image and markitdown on the Python side.
quote: LibreOffice: soffice for PPTX to PDF conversion. Poppler: pdftoppm for PDF to image conversion. Pandoc: Document text extraction with tracked changes.

claim: Claude Code's Read tool silently degrades on PDFs when poppler-utils is missing — a common gotcha in node:22-bookworm based containers where it is not pre-installed, so poppler-utils should be an explicit apt line in any YOLO Dockerfile.
quote: Read tool cannot find poppler-utils in headless (-p) mode

claim: HolyClaude's 'full' image lands at roughly 4 GB and the 'slim' variant around 2 GB — a realistic size budget for a YOLO container once chromium, Playwright browsers, LibreOffice/Pandoc/ffmpeg and a full Python+Node stack are all in one layer-cake.
quote: Bundles Chromium, Playwright, and a full dev toolchain in the full image (~4GB); slim variant (~2GB) without browser components.

claim: Anthropic's devcontainer layout splits concerns into devcontainer.json (VS Code/Codespaces settings and mounts), Dockerfile (image + tools), and init-firewall.sh (iptables egress allow-list) — a three-file pattern worth copying for any YOLO sandbox that wants persisted auth and restricted network.
quote: The devcontainer setup consists of three primary components: devcontainer.json, Dockerfile, and init-firewall.sh (which establishes network security rules).

claim: Claude Code loads skills from four scopes with priority enterprise > personal > project, and plugins get their own namespace; personal is ~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md and project is .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md.
quote:
| Personal   | `~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`            | All your projects              |
| Project    | `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`              | This project only              |
| Plugin     | `<plugin>/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`             | Where plugin is enabled        |

claim: Anthropic's reference devcontainer is the sanctioned place to run claude --dangerously-skip-permissions; the container's firewall and isolation are what make YOLO mode defensible.
quote: The container's enhanced security measures (isolation and firewall rules) allow you to run `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` to bypass permission prompts for unattended operation.

claim: Anthropic's first-party anthropics/skills repo ships document skills (docx, pdf, pptx, xlsx) plus example skills, and is installed via /plugin marketplace add anthropics/skills followed by /plugin install document-skills@anthropic-agent-skills.
quote:
/plugin marketplace add anthropics/skills

# Then install specific skill sets:
/plugin install document-skills@anthropic-agent-skills
/plugin install example-skills@anthropic-agent-skills

claim: obra/superpowers is distributed as a Claude Code plugin via /plugin install superpowers@claude-plugins-official (official marketplace) or /plugin marketplace add obra/superpowers-marketplace, and is multi-platform (Codex, Cursor, Gemini CLI, Copilot CLI, OpenCode).
quote:
**Claude Code (Official):** Install via Anthropic's marketplace with `/plugin install superpowers@claude-plugins-official`

**Claude Code (Superpowers Marketplace):** Register the marketplace first (`/plugin marketplace add obra/superpowers-marketplace`)

claim: The common community preload pattern is a plain git clone into ~/.claude/skills, e.g. git clone https://github.com/obra/superpowers ~/.claude/skills/superpowers, and Claude Code auto-detects new skill folders.
quote: To install obra's superpowers collection (20+ battle-tested skills), use: `git clone https://github.com/obra/superpowers ~/.claude/skills/superpowers`

claim: Docker itself ships a docker model skills --claude command that writes skill definitions into ~/.claude/skills (or a custom --dest), with --force to overwrite — useful as a Dockerfile RUN step.
quote:
Docker places Claude Code skills in `~/.claude/skills` when you use the `--claude` flag.

**Command Invocation:**
To install skills for Claude Code, you run:
```
docker model skills --claude
```

claim: Anthropic's official line is that Skills teach behavior (procedural knowledge, cheap tokens, on-demand) while MCP servers supply connectivity/tools (heavier, often tens of thousands of tokens); the two compose.
quote: Skills = procedural knowledge (30-50 tokens each, loaded on-demand) MCP = external tool connections (can use 50k+ tokens)

claim: The official anthropics/claude-plugins-official marketplace is auto-registered at startup, and /plugin, /plugin install <plugin>@<marketplace>, /reload-plugins are the interactive install path; plugins bundle skills, commands, subagents, MCP servers, and hooks together.
quote: The official Anthropic marketplace (claude-plugins-official) is automatically available when you start Claude Code... Plugins are a lightweight way to package and share any combination of: Slash commands, Subagents, MCP servers, and Hooks.

claim: On Linux and Windows Claude Code stores credentials in ~/.claude/.credentials.json (mode 0600), or under $CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR; macOS uses the encrypted Keychain.
quote: on macOS, credentials are stored in the encrypted macOS Keychain. On Linux and Windows, credentials are stored in `~/.claude/.credentials.json`, or under `$CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` if that variable is set. On Linux, the file is written with mode `0600`

claim: Bind-mounting only .credentials.json is not enough to survive container restarts — Claude Code re-prompts for login even with valid tokens present.
quote: OAuth authentication credentials are not persisting between Docker container runs, even though the credentials file (`.credentials.json`) is being saved and properly mounted.

claim: The working pattern is to authenticate in a setup container, capture the full ~/.claude directory, and mount it read-only into production containers with a per-run working copy.
quote: The authentication directory is mounted as a read-only volume in production environments. A working copy is created for each execution to prevent state conflicts between runs.

claim: OAuth login inside a devcontainer fails because the random ephemeral callback port isn't forwarded to the host browser; workarounds include pinning --callback-port and pre-declaring forwardPorts.
quote: Claude login starts a localhost callback server on a random ephemeral port. In devcontainers, this port isn't forwarded to the host, so the OAuth callback from the host browser never reaches Claude Code.

claim: Docker's own Claude Code sandbox keeps credentials out of the container by running the OAuth flow through an external proxy rather than writing files inside.
quote: If no API key is set, Claude Code prompts you to authenticate interactively using OAuth. The proxy handles the OAuth flow, so credentials aren't stored inside the sandbox.

claim: gh CLI prefers a system credential store but falls back to a plaintext file; for headless use, GH_TOKEN env var is recommended, especially for fine-grained PATs.
quote: an authentication token will be stored securely in the system credential store. If a credential store is not found or there is an issue using it gh will fallback to writing the token to a plain text file ... Favour setting `GH_TOKEN` for fine-grained personal access token usage

claim: GitHub recommends fine-grained PATs scoped to selected repositories with minimal permissions and an expiration date, rather than classic broad-scope PATs.
quote: Fine-grained PATs let you restrict access to only the repositories and permissions you specify and support setting expiration dates for added security.

claim: Docker Compose secrets are mounted as files under /run/secrets/<name> and are only visible to services that explicitly list them, unlike env-vars which leak into process listings and logs.
quote: Services can only access secrets when explicitly granted by a `secrets` attribute ... Docker creates a bind mount under `/run/secrets/<secret_name>` with their specific values

claim: In a live prompt-injection attack, `ps auxeww | grep` was used to dump ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and GITHUB_TOKEN from the Claude Code subprocess's inherited environment into a PR comment.
quote: The subprocess inheriting all environment variables was critical to the exploit ... the process retained access to `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` and `GITHUB_TOKEN` in its environment, making them accessible to any executed commands.

claim: CVE-2025-59536: a malicious .claude/settings.json can set ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL to an attacker endpoint, leaking the Authorization header bearing the API key before the user trusts the directory.
quote: an attacker could inject a malicious `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` environment variable into the repository's `.claude/settings.json` file ... Claude Code would route all API requests through the attacker's server "before the victim decides to trust the directory," exposing the authorization header

claim: Static API keys are bearer strings with no binding to user, device, or workload, so any prompt-injected process can exfiltrate and replay them with no revocation trail or scope.
quote: API keys are just strings. They can be embedded in documents, injected via prompts, copy-pasted anywhere, used by anyone who possesses them ... no binding between the API key and the legitimate user, device, or workload.

claim: Anthropic's official devcontainer ships an init-firewall.sh that sets iptables default-drop on INPUT/FORWARD/OUTPUT, allows DNS/SSH/loopback/established, then accepts egress only for an ipset 'allowed-domains' populated from registry.npmjs.org, api.anthropic.com, sentry.io, statsig.anthropic.com, marketplace.visualstudio.com, and GitHub CIDRs fetched from api.github.com/meta.
quote: iptables -P OUTPUT DROP ... iptables -A OUTPUT -m set --match-set allowed-domains dst -j ACCEPT ... iptables -A OUTPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp-admin-prohibited

claim: Claude Code's native /sandbox uses bubblewrap on Linux/WSL2 and Seatbelt on macOS for filesystem isolation, and routes egress through a proxy server that enforces a domain allowlist, with explicit warnings that allowing /var/run/docker.sock via allowUnixSockets grants full host access and that broad domains like github.com enable exfiltration.
quote: The `allowUnixSockets` configuration can inadvertently grant access to powerful system services ... if it is used to allow access to `/var/run/docker.sock` this would effectively grant access to the host system through exploiting the docker socket.

claim: Anthropic's engineering post states that effective sandboxing requires both filesystem and network isolation, because network-only isolation still lets an agent exfiltrate SSH keys and filesystem-only isolation still lets it reach exfil endpoints.
quote: effective sandboxing requires _both_ filesystem and network isolation. Without both: Network-only isolation allows exfiltration of sensitive files (SSH keys); Filesystem-only isolation permits escape and network access exploitation.

claim: Simon Willison's lethal-trifecta framing identifies the three conditions that make prompt-injection exfiltration possible — private-data access, untrusted content, and outbound comms — and argues sandboxing is the main lever to cut the trifecta by limiting what private data and exfil channels the agent has.
quote: The lethal trifecta for AI agents: access to your private data, exposure to untrusted content, and the ability to externally communicate in a way that could be used to steal your data (exfiltration).

claim: A widely-shared community reference implementation for Claude Code uses docker-compose with cap_add NET_ADMIN (only to let an entrypoint configure iptables at start), then drops to a non-root devuser, default-denies egress, and allowlists api.anthropic.com, claude.ai, registry.npmjs.org, and github.com.
quote: iptables -P INPUT DROP ... iptables -P OUTPUT DROP ... Claude installs as a non-root user ('devuser'), though the container entrypoint runs as root specifically for firewall initialization.

claim: The FoamoftheSea claude-code-sandbox layers defense-in-depth for --dangerously-skip-permissions mode: non-root devuser with sudo scoped to only lock-settings.sh and init-firewall.sh, root-owned read-only claude-settings.json so the agent cannot rewrite its own rules, pids/mem/cpu limits to stop fork bombs, and no Docker socket, SSH keys, or host credentials mounted.
quote: iptables default-deny policy blocks all outbound traffic except the Anthropic API and Docker-embedded DNS ... No Docker socket, no SSH keys, no host credentials mounted.

claim: Mounting /var/run/docker.sock is worse than --privileged because the container looks unprivileged to scanners yet can spawn unlimited new privileged containers; a one-liner like `docker run --privileged --pid=host -v /:/host ubuntu chroot /host bash` from inside the sandbox owns the host.
quote: docker run -it --rm --privileged --pid=host --net=host -v /:/host ubuntu:22.04 chroot /host bash ... The container looks completely normal (not privileged).

claim: The o1 system card and subsequent SandboxEscapeBench work from Oxford and the UK AI Security Institute show frontier LLMs can scan for and exploit exposed Docker APIs and misconfigurations from inside a sandbox, and recommend dropping CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_MODULE, CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH, and CAP_NET_RAW when deploying AI agents.
quote: the model discovered a misconfigured Docker API on the evaluation host, used network scanning to identify the exposed daemon, and exploited it to spawn containers and read protected files.

claim: Wiz's 2025 IMDS research shows attackers routinely pivot from SSRF or compromised workloads to 169.254.169.254 to steal temporary IAM credentials, and recommends blocking that endpoint (and IPv6 fd00:ec2::254) at the container network layer for any workload that doesn't need it.
quote: Track egress requests from workloads and APIs to private and internal IP ranges and cloud metadata IPs such as AWS 169.254.169.254 and IPv6 [fd00:ec2::254].

claim: The canonical AI-agent Docker hardening recipe combines --read-only (immutable rootfs), --tmpfs /tmp with noexec,nosuid, --security-opt=no-new-privileges, --cap-drop=ALL with targeted --cap-add only where needed, and --user 1000:1000 to avoid root in the container.
quote: --read-only (immutable filesystem prevents binary modification), --security-opt=no-new-privileges (blocks privilege escalation), --cap-drop=ALL (removes all Linux capabilities) ... running as a non-root user (1000:1000).

claim: For workloads where Docker namespace isolation feels insufficient, gVisor (user-space syscall interception), Kata Containers (lightweight VMs under a container API), and Firecracker microVMs (~100-200ms boot, KVM-enforced) are the three stronger-isolation options, with Firecracker considered the gold standard for running untrusted AI-generated code.
quote: Firecracker MicroVM's combination of hardware-level isolation plus millisecond startup plus extremely low overhead is widely recognized as the optimal gold standard for running untrusted AI code.

claim: For domain-level HTTPS allowlisting without MITM certificates, iron-proxy and Squid in SNI peek/splice mode inspect the TLS ClientHello and splice or reject based on an allowlist, which is the preferred pattern over mitmproxy (a debugger, not an enforcer) for production agent egress gateways.
quote: Iron-proxy is purpose-built for controlling and auditing egress from untrusted workloads. Mitmproxy is a great debugging tool but isn't designed for production enforcement.

claim: Anthropic's devcontainer.json does NOT pass --dangerously-skip-permissions; instead it adds NET_ADMIN/NET_RAW caps, mounts a volume at /home/node/.claude for auth persistence, and runs init-firewall.sh as postStartCommand.
quote: runArgs: [--cap-add=NET_ADMIN, --cap-add=NET_RAW] ... source=claude-code-config-${devcontainerId},target=/home/node/.claude,type=volume ... postStartCommand: sudo /usr/local/bin/init-firewall.sh

claim: HolyClaude is a single-maintainer (CoderLuii) AI coding workstation image with ~2.1k stars (April 2026, v1.2.2 released 2026-04-10) bundling 7 AI CLIs (Claude Code, Gemini, Codex, Cursor, TaskMaster, Junie, OpenCode) plus Node 22, Python 3, Playwright, Postgres/Redis/SQLite clients, FFmpeg, and a web UI on port 3001.
quote: AI coding workstation: Claude Code + web UI + 7 AI CLIs + headless browser + 50+ tools ... docker compose up -d ... http://localhost:3001

claim: claude-code-sandbox (textcortex) is archived as of February 2026 at ~308 stars; it was an npm CLI (claude-sandbox start) that ran Claude in Docker/Podman with files copied in (not mounted) and explicitly invoked --dangerously-skip-permissions with allowedTools defaulting to ['*'].
quote: This was an early PoC and is now archived. Follow Spritz for the continuation of this vision ... Claude runs with --dangerously-skip-permissions flag (safe in container)

claim: Sculptor by Imbue (~146 stars, still in active beta April 2026, released 2025-09-26) wraps Docker Desktop with a UI for running many parallel Claude Code agents in per-agent containers, using Mutagen for bidirectional 'Pairing Mode' sync between container and host IDE; OAuth sign-in for Pro/Max plans is post-beta.
quote: Sculptor is a UI for running parallel coding agents in safe, isolated sandboxes ... We plan on pushing out an update soon that allows Claude auth sign-in so you can use your Claude Pro and Max plans!

claim: Sculptor's defining feature is Pairing Mode, which one-clicks an agent's container work back into the developer's local repo while keeping files and git state synced bidirectionally, and currently runs on Mac (Apple Silicon) and Linux with Intel/Windows coming.
quote: With one click, Sculptor brings an agent's work from its container into your local repo, keeping your files and git state synced.

claim: Dagger's container-use (~3.7k stars, Go-based, v0.4.2 August 2025) is an MCP server that lets any MCP-compatible agent (Claude Code, Cursor) spin up per-task containers on a separate git branch via 'npx @anthropic-ai/claude-code mcp add container-use -- cu stdio'; environments are defined as Dagger composable functions, not static images.
quote: Each agent gets a fresh container in its own git branch - run multiple agents without conflicts, experiment safely, discard failures instantly.

claim: Docker Sandboxes (GA January 2026 on macOS/Windows, Linux coming) run each agent in a dedicated microVM with its own kernel and nested docker engine, launch Claude Code with --dangerously-skip-permissions by default, and support Claude Code, Copilot CLI, Codex, Gemini CLI, and Kiro out of the box.
quote: Each coding agent runs in a dedicated microVM with hypervisor-level security boundaries ... Host Docker daemon remains inaccessible to agents ... Unattended execution - No constant permission prompts

claim: claudebox by RchGrav (~1k stars, v2.0.0 July 2025) offers 15+ pre-baked profiles (C/C++, Python, Rust, Go, Java, ML, DevOps) with per-project isolation of Docker images, auth, and history under ~/.claudebox/<project>/.claude/, plus a project-specific firewall allowlist and an explicit --dangerously-skip-permissions flag.
quote: network isolation with project-specific firewall allowlists ... claudebox --dangerously-skip-permissions ... Authentication state persists per-project in ~/.claudebox/<project-name>/.claude/

claim: Among cloud alternatives to local Docker for Claude Code, E2B uses Firecracker microVMs with ~150ms cold starts, Daytona uses Docker containers with industry-leading 27-90ms provisioning (raised $24M Series A Feb 2026), and Modal runs sandboxes on gVisor defined dynamically at runtime.
quote: Daytona achieves fastest provisioning—27-90ms from request to ready ... E2B has 150ms Firecracker cold starts ... Modal ... sandboxes running on gVisor

claim: Anthropic's official Agent SDK hosting guide names Modal Sandbox, Cloudflare Sandboxes, Daytona, E2B, Fly Machines, and Vercel Sandbox as supported cloud runtimes for hosting Claude Code agents.
quote: Claude Code agents can be hosted on multiple sandbox platforms including Modal Sandbox, Cloudflare Sandboxes, Daytona, E2B, Fly Machines, and Vercel Sandbox.

claim: The ghcr.io/anthropics/devcontainer-features/claude-code devcontainer feature silently overwrites any user-customized /usr/local/bin/init-firewall.sh because features run after the Dockerfile build, a known documented footgun.
quote: the devcontainer feature ... installs its own init-firewall.sh to /usr/local/bin/init-firewall.sh, which silently overwrites any user-customized version

claim: Willison argues YOLO mode plus a remote sandbox is the right shape: 'the best sandboxes run on someone else's computer', and network restrictions cut off the data-exfiltration leg of the lethal trifecta.
quote: The best sandboxes run on someone else's computer... cuts off the data exfiltration leg of the lethal trifecta.

claim: The 'lethal trifecta' — private data access + untrusted content + external communication — is Willison's framing for why network isolation is the most important mitigation for YOLO agents.
quote: Anyone who gets text into your LLM has full control over what tools it runs next.

claim: Anthropic's March 2026 auto-mode post admits users approve 93% of permission prompts — the empirical basis for the 'permission fatigue makes prompts theatre' argument.
quote: Approval fatigue, where people stop paying close attention to what they're approving.

claim: Anthropic's security docs explicitly tell users to run Claude Code in VMs or devcontainers for untrusted content, and warn that no system is fully immune to prompt injection.
quote: Use virtual machines (VMs) to run scripts and make tool calls, especially when interacting with external web services... While these protections significantly reduce risk, no system is completely immune to all attacks.

claim: Anthropic's own docs contradict each other: Best Practices says YOLO requires 'a sandbox without internet access', but the Devcontainer docs say firewall rules are sufficient — the issue was closed 'not planned'.
quote: Only use --dangerously-skip-permissions in a sandbox without internet access... The container's enhanced security measures (isolation and firewall rules) allow you to run claude --dangerously-skip-permissions.

claim: UpGuard's audit of 18,470 public Claude config files found 22.2% granted Bash(rm:*), 21.3% granted curl, and only 1.1% used deny/ask rules — evidence that the 'permissions' layer is mostly performative.
quote: A shocking 22.2% of files (4,101 users) granted the Bash(rm:*) permission... Only 1.1% of files used [deny and ask rules].

claim: Multiple documented 2025 incidents of Claude Code running destructive rm -rf that hit users' home directories via shell tilde expansion — the canonical 'sandbox would have saved me' case.
quote: rm -rf tests/ patches/ plan/ ~/ - See that ~/ at the end? That's your entire home directory.

claim: Invariant Labs' May 2025 GitHub MCP prompt-injection proved an agent with broad PAT access can be hijacked by a public issue into leaking private repo contents via a public PR.
quote: When a developer innocently asks their AI assistant to 'check the open issues,' the agent reads the malicious issue, gets prompt-injected, and follows hidden instructions to access private repositories and leak sensitive data publicly.

claim: Cyata found three CVEs (CVE-2025-68143/68144/68145) in Anthropic's own Git MCP server, chainable to RCE via git smudge/clean filters — the 'toxic combination' problem with MCP composability.
quote: Each MCP server might look safe in isolation, but combine two of them... and you get a toxic combination.

claim: April 2026 Register coverage: researchers say MCP's STDIO handling is an architectural flaw enabling unauthenticated command injection and zero-click prompt injection across 200k+ servers; Anthropic declined to change the protocol, calling the behavior 'expected'.
quote: it actually lets anyone run any arbitrary OS command, if the command successfully creates an STDIO server it will return the handle.

claim: Trail of Bits' October 2025 research demonstrated prompt-injection-to-RCE against three agent platforms including Claude Code, bypassing human approval gates — evidence that per-action prompts alone are not a security boundary.
quote: bypassed human approval protections for system command execution in AI agents, achieving RCE in three agent platforms.

claim: March 2026 community research synthesises the 2026 consensus: the sandbox is the only layer that catches novel attacks because it prevents the outcome rather than recognising the command, while deny rules are 'a friction layer, not a security boundary'.
quote: The sandbox is the only layer that catches novel attacks — because it doesn't need to recognize the command, it prevents the outcome... Deny rules are a friction layer, not a security boundary.

claim: HolyClaude's quick-start docker-compose.yaml grants cap_add SYS_ADMIN and SYS_PTRACE plus security_opt seccomp=unconfined, with no cap_drop, no privileged, no read_only, and no user directive.
quote:
cap_add:
      - SYS_ADMIN      # Required for Chromium sandboxing
      - SYS_PTRACE      # Required for debugging
    security_opt:
      - seccomp=unconfined  # Required for Chromium in Docker

claim: HolyClaude's full-configuration compose file uses the same SYS_ADMIN + SYS_PTRACE + seccomp=unconfined posture and passes PUID/PGID=1000 as env vars rather than using compose's user: directive.
quote:
cap_add:
      - SYS_ADMIN
      - SYS_PTRACE
    security_opt:
      - seccomp=unconfined
...
      - PUID=1000
      - PGID=1000

claim: Docker's default seccomp profile is an allowlist that blocks around 44 syscalls out of 300+, including keyctl/add_key/request_key (keyring not namespaced), ptrace, mount/umount, unshare/clone/setns, kexec_load, reboot and io_uring_*.
quote: The default seccomp profile provides a sane default for running containers with seccomp and disables around 44 system calls out of 300+.

claim: Datadog Security Labs characterizes Docker's default seccomp profile as providing 'a good level of isolation' and credits it with blocking historical container-breakout exploits such as CVE-2022-0185 (unshare-based).
quote: the Docker default seccomp profile provides a good level of isolation ... has been instrumental in blocking some exploits that may otherwise have allowed for container breakouts

claim: A demonstrated escape against seccomp=unconfined loads a malicious kernel module via finit_module, which the default profile blocks; the attack requires CAP_SYS_MODULE, so cap-drop=ALL would neutralize this specific vector.
quote: this failed even though I had CAP_SYS_MODULE ... [after seccomp=unconfined] insert the module into the kernel

claim: OWASP's Docker cheat sheet recommends starting from --cap-drop=ALL and adding only what's needed, explicitly framing capability restriction and seccomp as complementary defense-in-depth layers rather than either one being sufficient alone.
quote: Always start with --cap-drop=ALL and add only the capabilities your application actually needs ... combine capability restrictions with other security measures like non-root users, read-only filesystems, and seccomp profiles for defense in depth

claim: Docker community forum guidance on the exact SYS_PTRACE + seccomp=unconfined combo HolyClaude uses advises starting from the default seccomp policy and removing only the specific syscalls the app needs, rather than disabling seccomp wholesale.
quote: the best approach would be to start from Docker's default seccomp policy and then just remove the relevant syscalls needed for your application

claim: The docx skill in anthropics/skills is NOT Apache 2.0 — it is governed by Anthropic's Consumer/Commercial Terms of Service, prohibiting extraction, redistribution, derivative works, and reverse engineering.
quote: Extract these materials from the Services or retain copies of these materials outside the Services... Reproducing or copying materials beyond temporary automatic copies during authorized use... Creating derivative works... Distributing, sublicensing, or transferring materials to third parties... Reverse engineering, decompiling, or disassembling

claim: Claude Code v2.1.0 introduced Skill hot-reload: skills in ~/.claude/skills or .claude/skills become immediately available without restart.
quote: Skill hot-reload - Skills in `~/.claude/skills` or `.claude/skills` immediately available without restart

claim: Claude OAuth access tokens are short-lived (roughly one hour) and rely on a refresh token for renewal, which can break autonomous agents mid-run, while static API keys do not expire unless manually rotated.
quote: Claude OAuth access tokens are short-lived and expire after a set period — typically within an hour... The Anthropic API with a persistent API key is the only reliable solution for unattended operation, since API keys don't expire unless you manually rotate them.
